parts.content: Explain the concept of perspective in relation to your class practical that used an interview or focus group.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying perspective in interviews (influence of participant or researcher viewpoint).

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining how perspective affects interpretation of qualitative data.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for linking this to their own practical interview/focus group.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for accurate psychological terminology and clarity.

parts.content: Describe how you used a survey/questionnaire in your class practical, including aim and procedure.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for correctly identifying the method as a survey or questionnaire.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for a clear statement of the aim of the survey/questionnaire.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for describing one aspect of the procedure (e.g., format of questions, sample size, distribution method).

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for further detail about the procedure (e.g., closed vs open questions, anonymity, data collection process).

parts.content: Design an interview or focus group to investigate the same topic as you investigated in your class practical that used a survey/questionnaire.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of interview/focus group design, including aim, guiding questions, and procedures.

1–2 marks: Minimal description. Aim vague, no example questions, or little sense of how data would be collected.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description. Aim described, some guiding questions included but limited detail. Some mention of procedure.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed design. Aim specific, guiding questions linked to the topic, procedure includes setting, ethical safeguards, and how responses will be recorded. Strong link to the survey topic.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Stating the aim.
Suggesting interview questions.
Procedure: semi-structured format, group of 5–8 participants, informed consent, anonymity assured, audio recording with transcription.

parts.content: Design a survey/questionnaire to investigate the same topic as you investigated in your class practical that used an interview or focus group.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of survey design, including clear aim, question types, and ethical considerations.

1–2 marks: Minimal description of a questionnaire. Aim vague, no example questions.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description. Aim described, some relevant questions included but limited detail. Procedure partly explained.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed design. Aim specific, a range of question types (Likert scales, closed/open items) included, ethical issues considered. Strong link to the focus group/interview topic.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Stating an aim (e.g. investigating attitudes toward cultural adaptation among immigrant adolescents).
Identifying elements of the survey/questionnaire; likert scales, or open-ended questions.
Procedure: anonymous completion, confidentiality explained, standardised instructions.

questions.specification: A driving-safety researcher tests whether phone use while driving affects working memory. Participants complete a simulated driving task, with and without a phone, while researchers record errors and reaction times.

parts.content: Explain **one** strength and **one** limitation of using an experiment.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for accurate identification and explanation of a strength and a limitation of the experimental method, with application to the scenario.

1–2 marks: Limited description of **one** or both points. Application is minimal.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description of a strength and limitation. Application is partial but relevant.

5–6 marks: Accurate and detailed explanation of both strength and limitation. Strong application to the driving-safety experiment.

**Acceptable responses:**
Strength: High control of variables (simulated driving task allows isolation of phone use as IV).
Limitation: Low ecological validity (simulator may not reflect real-world driving behaviour).

parts.content: Explain the concept of measurement in relation to your class practical that used a survey/questionnaire.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying measurement issues (e.g., validity of self-report, reliability of scales).

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining why measurement matters in surveys.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for linking this to their own practical survey/questionnaire.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for accurate terminology and clarity.

parts.content: Explain the concept of perspective in relation to your class practical that used an observation.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying perspective as the influence of viewpoint/interpretation on data.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining how perspective shapes observation outcomes.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for linking this to their own practical observation.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for accurate psychological terminology and clarity.

parts.content: Describe how you used an experiment in your class practical, including aim and procedure.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for correctly identifying the method as an experiment.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for a clear statement of the aim of the experiment.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for describing one key element of the procedure (e.g., participants, materials, or conditions).

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for further detail in the procedure showing how the experiment was conducted.

parts.content: Compare and contrast the use of a survey/questionnaire with an experiment.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of both methods and clear comparison.

1–2 marks: Limited description of survey and/or experiment. Comparisons are superficial or missing. Minimal connection to the practical.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description of both methods. At least one similarity or one difference is described, but not fully explained. Partial application to the practical.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed explanation of both methods. At least one similarity and one difference are explained and developed. Strong application to the practical, with accurate terminology.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Both can collect large amounts of data.
Experiments test causality, surveys gather correlations or perceptions.
Surveys are cost-efficient and flexible; experiments need more control and resources.
Experiments reduce self-report bias; surveys are more vulnerable to it.

parts.content: Explain the concept of causality in relation to your class practical that used an experiment.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying causality as the ability to show one variable directly influences another.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining how experiments establish causality (manipulation and control).

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for linking this to their own practical experiment.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for clear and accurate use of psychological terminology.

parts.content: Compare and contrast the use of observation in your class practical with a survey/questionnaire.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of both methods and clear comparison.

1–2 marks: Limited description of observation and/or survey. Comparisons are superficial or missing. Application to the practical is vague or absent.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description of both methods. At least one similarity or one difference is described but not fully developed. Partial link to the practical.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed explanation of both methods. At least one similarity and one difference are explained and developed. Strong application to the practical, with accurate use of terminology.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Both are non-experimental methods of data collection.
Observation records behaviour directly; surveys rely on self-report.
Observations may capture natural behaviour but are harder to replicate.
Surveys are easier to distribute but may suffer from bias (e.g., social desirability).

parts.content: Explain the concept of bias in relation to your class practical that used an experiment.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a type of bias in experiments (e.g., demand characteristics, researcher expectancy).

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining how this bias could influence results.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for linking the bias specifically to their own practical experiment.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for clarity and accuracy.

parts.content: Explain the concept of bias in relation to your class practical that used a survey/questionnaire.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying types of bias in surveys (e.g., social desirability, leading questions).

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining how this bias can distort responses.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for linking the bias to their own practical survey/questionnaire.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for accurate terminology and clear expression.

parts.content: Explain the concept of measurement in relation to your class practical that used an experiment.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying measurement issues (e.g., operational definitions, reliability).

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining why measurement is important in experiments.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for linking this to their own practical experiment.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for accurate terminology and clarity.

questions.specification: Sports scientists use fMRI scans to study how elite athletes make quick decisions during simulated competitions. The team hopes that brain imaging will reveal the neural basis of decision-making under pressure.

parts.content: Explain **one** strength and **one** limitation of fMRI in studying athletes' decision-making.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for accurate identification and explanation of a strength and limitation of fMRI.

1–2 marks: Limited description of **one** or both points. Minimal application.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description of strengths/limitations (e.g., “fMRI shows brain activity but is expensive”). Partial application.

5–6 marks: Detailed explanation of both strength and limitation with application to studying athletes.

**Acceptable responses:**
Strength: High spatial resolution → shows which brain areas are active during fast decisions.
Limitation: Low ecological validity → simulated competitions may not fully represent real-world pressure.

parts.content: Describe how you used an observation in your class practical, including aim and procedure.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for correctly identifying the method as an observation.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for a clear statement of the aim of the observation.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for describing one aspect of the procedure (e.g., setting, type of observation such as overt/covert, or what behaviour was observed).

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for additional detail in the procedure (e.g., recording method, duration, sampling technique).

parts.content: Explain the concept of responsibility in relation to your class practical that used an interview or focus group.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying researcher responsibility (e.g., confidentiality, protection from harm).

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining why responsibility is important in interview/focus group settings.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for linking this to their own practical interview/focus group.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for accurate terminology and clear explanation.

parts.content: Explain the concept of bias in relation to your class practical that used an observation.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying a type of bias that may occur in observations (e.g., observer bias, participant reactivity).

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining how this bias could influence results.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for linking the bias specifically to their own practical observation.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for accuracy and clarity of explanation.

parts.content: Compare and contrast the use of observation in your class practical with an experiment.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of both methods and clear comparison.

1–2 marks: Limited description of observation and/or experiment. Comparisons are superficial or missing. Minimal connection to the practical.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description of both methods. At least one similarity or one difference is described but not fully explained. Some application to the practical.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed explanation of both methods. At least one similarity and one difference are fully explained. Well-developed application to the practical, with correct terminology.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Experiments manipulate an independent variable, observations do not.
Experiments allow causal inferences; observations identify patterns or correlations.
Experiments are often more controlled; observations may have greater ecological validity.

questions.specification: In a multicultural classroom, a researcher observes how students from different backgrounds respond to group discussions. Some speak freely, while others hesitate, possibly due to cultural differences in communication norms.

parts.content: Explain **one** strength and **one** limitation of using observation.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of a relevant study and its application.

1–2 marks: Limited description of strengths/limitations of observation. Minimal application to scenario.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description of a strength and limitation. Partial application to cultural differences in classroom discussions.

5–6 marks: Accurate and detailed explanation of a strength and limitation with clear application to observing multicultural settings.

**Acceptable responses:**
Strength: Naturalistic setting provides real-life behaviours.
Limitation: Observer bias or misinterpretation of cultural behaviours.

parts.content: Compare and contrast the use of observation in your class practical with a focus group or interview.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of both methods and clear comparison.

1–2 marks: Limited description of observation and/or interview. Comparisons are minimal or absent. Application to the practical is vague.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description of both methods. At least one similarity or one difference is given, though not fully developed. Partial application to the practical.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed explanation of both methods. At least one similarity and one difference are explained and developed. Strong application to the practical, with correct terminology.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Both generate qualitative data useful for interpretation.
Observations capture behaviour, interviews gather self-reported thoughts.
Observations avoid demand characteristics; interviews risk social desirability or leading questions.
Interviews provide insight into participants’ perspectives, which observations alone cannot.

parts.content: Describe how you used an interview or focus group in your class practical, including aim and procedure.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for correctly identifying the method as an interview or focus group.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for a clear statement of the aim of the interview/focus group.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for describing one aspect of the procedure (e.g., type of questions, group size, or participant selection).

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for further detail about how the interview/focus group was conducted (e.g., structured vs semi-structured, recording method, duration).

parts.content: Design an observation to investigate the same topic as you investigated in your class practical that used an experiment.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of observational design, including clear aim, behaviours to be observed, and sampling method.

1–2 marks: Minimal reference to an observation. Aim vague. No mention of what behaviours would be observed.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description. Aim described, behaviours identified but not clearly operationalised. Some detail on procedure but limited structure.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed design. Aim is specific, behaviours operationalised, observation type (naturalistic/controlled, overt/covert, structured/unstructured) specified. Sampling and recording methods described.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Stating an aim (e.g. investigating whether students are more likely to collaborate when seated in groups vs. rows).
Identifying a procedure (e.g. structured observation with a checklist, inter-rater reliability, naturalistic setting) or research categories (e.g. instances of helping, sharing materials, verbal collaboration)
Ethical considerations (e.g., informed consent).

parts.content: Compare and contrast the use of a survey/questionnaire with a focus group or interview.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of both methods and clear comparison.

1–2 marks: Limited description of survey and/or interview. Comparisons are minimal or absent. Application to the practical is vague.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description of both methods. At least one similarity or one difference is described, though not fully developed. Partial application to the practical.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed explanation of both methods. At least one similarity and one difference are explained and developed. Strong application to the practical, with accurate terminology.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Both rely on self-report.
Surveys collect quantitative data; interviews generate qualitative data.
Surveys are efficient for large samples; interviews provide depth with fewer participants.
Interviews allow clarification of answers, while surveys are more structured and standardised.

parts.content: Compare and contrast the use of an experiment with a focus group or interview.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of both methods and clear comparison.

1–2 marks: Limited description of experiment and/or interview. Comparisons are minimal or absent. Minimal link to the practical.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description of both methods. At least one similarity or one difference is described, though not fully developed. Partial application to the practical.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed explanation of both methods. At least one similarity and one difference are explained and developed. Strong application to the practical, with accurate terminology.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Both can be used to investigate psychological phenomena.
Experiments produce quantitative, causal data; interviews provide qualitative, descriptive data.
Experiments have high control and replicability; interviews give insight into participant perspectives.
Interviews risk researcher bias; experiments risk low ecological validity.

parts.content: Explain the concept of causality in relation to your class practical that used an interview or focus group.
parts.markscheme:
Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for identifying that causality cannot usually be inferred from interviews/focus groups.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for explaining why (e.g., no manipulation/control, descriptive data).

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for linking this limitation to their own practical interview/focus group.

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for accurate terminology and clarity.

parts.content: Design an experiment to investigate the same topic as you investigated in your class practical that used a survey/questionnaire.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of experimental design and ability to adapt a self-report topic into an experiment.

1–2 marks: Minimal reference to an experiment. Aim vague, no IV/DV clearly identified.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description. Aim described, IV and DV partly operationalised. Procedure has limited control.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed design. Aim, IV, and DV are well-defined and operationalised. Procedure includes sample, controls, and ethical safeguards. Strong link to the original survey topic.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Stating an aim (e.g., investigating how peer presence affects helping behaviour).
Identifying an IV (peer presence vs. alone) and a DV (number of times participants help).
Outlining procedure: random assignment, controlled environment, debriefing.
Ethical considerations (e.g., informed consent, minimising deception).

parts.content: Design an experiment to investigate the same topic as you investigated in your class practical that used an observation.
parts.markscheme:
Award up to <marks>6 marks</marks> for knowledge of how to design an experiment, including clear aim, IV, DV, and procedure.

1–2 marks: Minimal description of an experiment. Aim or procedure mentioned vaguely. Lacks detail about variables or controls.

3–4 marks: Some accurate description of an experiment. Aim and procedure are described with partial detail. Some attempt at IV/DV, but not fully developed.

5–6 marks: Clear and detailed design of an experiment. Aim is specific, IV and DV are identified and operationalised, and procedure includes control of extraneous variables. Strong application to the same topic investigated in the observation.

**Acceptable responses may include:**
Stating an aim (e.g., investigating how peer presence affects helping behaviour).
Identifying an IV (peer presence vs. alone) and a DV (number of times participants help).
Outlining procedure: random assignment, controlled environment, debriefing.
Ethical considerations (e.g., informed consent, minimising deception).
